perturbation.original_text: As used herein, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" shall mean any and all technical and non-technical information provided by either party to the other, including but not limited to (a) patent and patent applications, (b) trade secret, and (c) proprietary information, ideas, techniques, sketches, drawings, works of authorship, models, inventions, know-how, processes, apparatuses, equipment, algorithms, software programs, software source documents, and formulae related to the current, future, and proposed products and services of each of the parties, and including, without limitation, their respective information concerning research, experimental work, development, design details and specifications, engineering, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, marketing plans and information the disclosing party provides regarding third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: As used herein, "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" shall mean some information provided by either party to the other.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the detailed definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' in Section 1 creates an in-text contradiction. While the agreement continues to refer to 'Confidential Information' throughout (e.g., Sections 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 19), the lack of a clear definition now introduces significant ambiguity, rendering the enforcement of clauses relating to the use, protection, and return of 'Confidential Information' uncertain. The phrase 'some information' is too vague.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party agrees that at all times until termination or expiration of this Agreement it will hold in strict confidence and not disclose to any third party Confidential Information of the other, except as approved in writing by the other party to this Agreement, and will use the Confidential Information for no purpose other than evaluating or pursuing a business relationship with the other party to this Agreement. Notwithstanding the above, the party to whom Confidential Information was disclosed (the "RECIPIENT") shall not be in violation of this Section 3 with regard to a disclosure that was in response to a valid order by a court or other governmental body, provided that the Recipient provides the other party with prior written notice of such disclosure in order to permit the other party to seek confidential treatment of such information. Each party shall only permit access to Confidential Information of the other party to those of its employees or authorized representatives having a need to know and who have signed confidentiality agreements or are otherwise bound by confidentiality obligations at least as restrictive as those contained herein.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a detailed definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION'. The changed text significantly weakens this definition to 'some information,' creating a strong ambiguity. The contradicted text relies on a clear definition of 'Confidential Information' to enforce obligations regarding its use and protection. The phrase 'some information' is far too vague and broadens the interpretation of confidential information, thus introducing a significant risk of it being interpreted differently from its original intent. Due to this difference, a direct contradiction is present.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall immediately notify the other upon discovery of any loss or unauthorized disclosure of the Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall immediately notify the other upon discovery of any loss or unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'of the Confidential Information of the other party' from Section 3, the requirement to notify the other party upon discovery of loss or unauthorized disclosure becomes ambiguous. It's no longer clear what type of information loss triggers the notification obligation. This weakens the agreement's protective measures and contradicts the intent to safeguard specific information defined as 'Confidential Information' elsewhere, particularly its now vague definition in Section 1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party shall immediately notify the other upon discovery of any loss or unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires notification upon discovery of loss or unauthorized disclosure of the *Confidential Information of the other party*. The changed text removes 'of the Confidential Information of the other party', broadening the notification requirement to any loss or unauthorized disclosure, regardless of whether it pertains to confidential information as defined in the contract. This contradicts the implicit scope and protection afforded to 'Confidential Information' as defined elsewhere, leading to a significant inconsistency in the contract's intent and enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: Each party's obligations under this Agreement with respect to any portion of the other party's Confidential Information shall terminate when the Recipient can document that: (a) it was in the public domain at the time it was <PAGE> 2 communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (b) it entered the public domain subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party through no fault of the Recipient; (c) it was in the Recipient's possession free of any obligation of confidence at the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (d) it was rightfully communicated to the Recipient free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party or (e) it was communicated by the other party to an unaffiliated third party free of any obligation of confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party's obligations under this Agreement with respect to information shall terminate when the Recipient can document that: (a) it was in the public domain at the time it was <PAGE> 2 communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (b) it entered the public domain subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party through no fault of the Recipient; (c) it was in the Recipient's possession free of any obligation of confidence at the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (d) it was rightfully communicated to the Recipient free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party or (e) it was communicated by the other party to an unaffiliated third party free of any obligation of confidence.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Confidential' from 'Confidential Information' in Section 4, the obligations termination clause becomes overly broad and contradictory. Since 'Confidential Information' is vaguely defined as 'some information' in Section 1, the clause now suggests that obligations terminate for *any* information, not just the intended sensitive data. This contradicts the core purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party's obligations under this Agreement with respect to information shall terminate when the Recipient can document that: (a) it was in the public domain at the time it was <PAGE> 2 communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (b) it entered the public domain subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party through no fault of the Recipient; (c) it was in the Recipient's possession free of any obligation of confidence at the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party; (d) it was rightfully communicated to the Recipient free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was communicated to the Recipient by the other party or (e) it was communicated by the other party to an unaffiliated third party free of any obligation of confidence.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information,' limiting the termination of obligations to specific types of information. The changed text broadens this to 'information,' potentially including all information exchanged, not just confidential. This contradicts the original intent and scope of the confidentiality agreement, creating a strong in-text contradiction as the obligations now terminate for a much broader set of data, as explained in the perturbation explanation. The changed text is effectively redefining the scope of the agreement in a way that is incompatible with the original terms.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information contained in the Confidential Information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'representing the other's Confidential Information and all copies thereof' and 'contained in the Confidential Information' from Section 5 introduces ambiguity and contradiction. The obligation to destroy or return materials is now undefined, since it's unclear what materials must be destroyed. The modified text suggests destroying 'all documents' without qualifying that these documents should be 'Confidential Information' and it also removes the context from which the information should be destroyed, which is the Confidential Information, contradicting the purpose of the NDA and making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, or upon written request of the other party, each party shall promptly destroy or return to the other all documents and other tangible materials. The Recipient agrees to destroy all documents, memoranda, notes and other writings whatsoever prepared by the Recipient or its employees or representatives based on the information (except for references or summaries appearing in minutes or corporate records).
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the crucial specification that the destroyed or returned documents must relate to the other party's 'Confidential Information'. This creates a contradiction because the modified text implies a potentially unlimited scope of destruction, whereas the original text and contradicted text intend to limit the destruction obligation to confidential materials. The modified text no longer clearly aligns with the intended scope of the original agreement, as it broadens the obligation beyond Confidential Information, thereby creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Although the disclosing party has endeavored to include in the Confidential Information, information known to it which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of the Recipient's investigation of a potential transaction, the Recipient acknowledges and agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives have made or make any representations or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of all or any portion of the Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives shall have any liability to the Recipient or any of the Recipient's employees or representatives resulting from the use of, or conclusions arising from, the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Although the disclosing party has endeavored to include information known to it which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of the Recipient's investigation of a potential transaction, the Recipient acknowledges and agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives have made or make any representations or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of all or any portion of the information. The Recipient agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives shall have any liability to the Recipient or any of the Recipient's employees or representatives resulting from the use of, or conclusions arising from, the information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'in the Confidential Information', and replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'information' in Section 7, the disclaimer of liability becomes overly broad. Because the definition of 'Confidential Information' is now 'some information', this section now implies that the disclosing party bears no responsibility for any information provided, which goes against the intended purpose of the agreement to protect certain sensitive details, therefore, creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Although the disclosing party has endeavored to include information known to it which it believes to be relevant for the purpose of the Recipient's investigation of a potential transaction, the Recipient acknowledges and agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives have made or make any representations or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of all or any portion of the information. The Recipient agrees that neither the disclosing party nor any of its employees or representatives shall have any liability to the Recipient or any of the Recipient's employees or representatives resulting from the use of, or conclusions arising from, the information.
perturbation.justification: The original text limits the disclaimer of liability to 'Confidential Information'. The changed text broadens this disclaimer to 'information', removing the link to 'Confidential Information' and thus contradicting the purpose of the contract, which is to protect specific confidential details. The contradicted text now implies that the disclosing party bears no responsibility for any information provided, thereby creating a direct conflict with the intended purpose and scope of the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from any Confidential Information to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party shall make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from information to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 8, by deleting 'Confidential' the clause is now contradictory since the term 'Confidential Information' still exists in Section 2. The removal of the critical modifier broadens the scope of the restriction to *any* information, which undermines the agreement's targeted protection of only sensitive data. This contrasts with the still-present intent to protect specific, confidential information elsewhere in the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Neither party shall make, have made, use or sell for any purpose any product or other item using, incorporating or derived from information to the other party.
perturbation.justification: The original text restricted the use of 'Confidential Information'. The changed text removes 'Confidential', broadening the restriction to *any* information. The 'Contradicted Text' is the same as the 'Changed Text'. Since the original agreement aimed to protect only sensitive data, this creates a contradiction because the contract still refers to 'Confidential Information' elsewhere, creating a conflict. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists.
